data.title: Powell And Bessent Summon Bank CEOs For An 'Urgent' Meeting - What's Going On
data.description:
An "urgent" meeting

This news hit the market unexpectedly on Friday morning: Bessent, Powell convened urgent meeting with bank CEOs about Anthropic's new AI model.

So, this meeting was convened by 1) the Fed Chair, who is in charge of monetary policy with emergency powers to respond to a major financial crisis to protect the financial markets and liquidity, 2) the Treasury Department Secretary, who is in charge of fiscal policy and responsible for the US Dollar stability.

The meeting included the CEOs of major financial institutions, basically in charge of the global financial system.

This was not a seminar on AI, or a lower-level meeting on technology - this was the highest-level meeting, and it was "urgent", as specifically pointed out by the media.

Usually, these types of highest-level urgent meetings are held in response to a major systematic risk threatening the financial system.

So, what was so urgent, and important to have the highest-level policymakers and finance executives to meet?

The media says it was about the new Anthropic AI model internally called Mythoss, still not released to the public.

The new Anthropic model Mythoss

There is definitely uncertainty why would the new AI model require an urgent meeting at the highest level.

The available information suggests this is not a rogue agentic AI model, with a potential to disrupt the global economy, and it's not a cyber-attack.

Mythos seems to be "an advanced large‑language/reasoning model developed by Anthropic, which is more autonomous and more capable than standard chat-style models, currently evaluated or tested for high‑stakes financial use cases (risk management, strategy, decision support, possibly trading or credit decisions)"

The keyword here is "reasoning model'.

Thus, Mythos is an advanced reasoning tool developed for finance, but it's not more advanced based on quantitative analysis, it's more advanced on providing a coherent narrative.

So, the key is not "intelligence" — it’s "persuasive synthesis", or integrating macro data, market data, geopolitical events, and financial data to generate coherent, narrative‑driven actionable ideas.

Thus, it's the narrative that matters - and markets generally move on narrative.

Why is this important?

In my opinion, this is not only about Anthropic, all advanced LLMs ALSO have reasoning capabilities to create persuasive narratives based on data, with effective prompt engineering.

The problem is that financial institutions are increasingly adopting LLMs for decision making, specifically to analyze the relevant data and to create actionable narratives or themes.

Thus, if all financial institutions use these LLMs to create narratives using the same data and information, then all financial institutions will arrive at the same narrative.

That's a major problem if there is a brewing financial crisis - advanced reasoning LLMs will detect the crisis using probability scenarios, and thus, all financial institutions will act at once on this information. During the financial crisis, this means perfect correlations and liquidity shock - that becomes a systemic risk issue.

The Fed and the Treasury are concerned about systemic events - thus, a reasoning model like Mythos is a systemic risk. That explains the meeting.

Why now, why is it urgent?

The fact that AI can create crowded trades and increase market volatility is a well-known risk. So, there is nothing new here really to require an urgent highest-level meeting.

Mythoss is more dangerous than other quantitative, algorithmic models like VaR because these models compress risk into numbers and probabilities, while Mythoss compresses risk into a story, a narrative, and humans are more likely to believe a story.

In my opinion, this meeting is urgent because the global economy could be facing a depression - a very severe inflationary recession with a significant stock market crash, and it could be imminent. Specifically, we are facing an energy price shock due to the war in Iran - this is exogenous shock.

The advanced reasoning AI models used by banks could be viewed as an endogenous amplifier - and this could make the crisis much worse.

Given that the US and Iran will have negotiations this weekend, and that the likelihood of compromise is very low, the unfolding energy shock could create a global depression.

The situation in Iran is exactly the type of a situation that an advanced reasoning AI model can be used to create an actionable narrative, since there is no data to act upon it.

A global depression

I pointed to the Fed's global adverse scenario for 2026 - it's basically an inflationary shock due to higher oil prices, with a significant stock market crash, higher interest rates, wider credit spreads, and stronger dollar. That's exactly what's unfolding.

Here is how the current situation could trigger a global depression:

  • Higher oil price triggers inflationary shock.
  • Oil supply shock causes rationing, closing of airports, factories, businesses..., and these further complicate supply chains and increase inflation and lower growth.
  • Higher interest rates trigger a housing bubble burst in the US and globally.
  • The stock market bubble bursts with stagflation, as AI bubble bursts.
  • Due to the negative wealth effect, the recession gets much deeper.
  • Higher interest rates and a recession burst the private credit bubble, and CRE bubble, causing a systemic credit event and exposing the financial sector.

Taken all together, the global economy enters a deep recession triggered by the inflationary shock.

So, this is my narrative. Also, this is Fed's global adverse scenario for 2026. Now, what's the Wall Street narrative? It's not going to matter. Anybody with domain expertise can input these events into any copilot - and get the narrative. If the narrative is persuasive enough, the market participants could act on it - and that's how the crisis starts.

Implications

There are two competing narratives with the respect to the current energy price spike with the likely effects on economy.

One (benign) narrative is that the war in Iran will end soon and that energy prices will collapse as soon as the war is over, with transitory effects on economy.

The other side is saying that the this is likely a long-term problem for energy supplies if Iran keeps the control of Hormuz and continues to develop nuclear weapons, with more persistent inflationary pressures and negative effects on economy.

The stock market (SP500) has been trading based on headlines with a clear bias towards the benign narrative, while the bond market (US2Y) (US10Y) is still biased to price a Fed cut, and still pricing an anchored inflation expectations - also favoring the benign narrative.

The Fed and the Treasury are possibly concerned that the advanced reasoning AI models could tip the narrative in the undesirable direction and trigger a systemic risk event. That possibly explains the urgent meeting, before the negotiating over the weekend in Pakistan.

So, how should investors adjust their portfolios? The high-level meeting can be viewed as a warning consistent with the Fed's global adverse scenario - which calls for lower stock prices, higher interest rates, and stronger USD. Thus, my recommendation is to increase allocation to Short-term Treasury Bills, 1-3 month duration, reinvest if the Fed hikes interest rates.

data.title: Snowflake Intelligence Is Here: How AI Is Supercharging The Agentic Trajectory
data.description:
It is finally clear that Snowflake Inc. (SNOW) is an AI winner. The only problem? Software has seemingly become one of the most hated sectors in the market as investors worry about disruption risk from every new AI model such as Claude Mythos. This means that even as SNOW is posting enviable and accelerating top-line growth, it now trades at among its most reasonable valuations in recent history. The stock still commands a generous premium to peers, but directionally this looks justified. I am upgrading SNOW stock to a Buy rating.

SNOW Stock Price

I last covered SNOW in January, where I cautioned that the company may be gearing towards a profitability pivot at the expense of growth. The stock has plummeted since.

Growth has accelerated higher, and even with the rest of software imploding, it is time for an upgrade.

SNOW Stock Key Metrics

SNOW is an enterprise software company helping customers to process and use their data. It has taken a while, but the company has finally seen its growth accelerate, helping to bridge the intuitive notion that data is made more valuable due to AI.

In the most recent quarter, SNOW generated $1.227 billion in product revenue, representing 30% YoY growth, and crushing guidance of between $1.195 billion to $1.2 billion.

The company has seen its net revenue retention rate stabilize at 125%.

SNOW saw non-GAAP operating margins jump 200 bps and free cash flow margins jump an astonishing 1,800 bps.

The company however has not made meaningful progress towards GAAP profitability. Yet with revenue growth back to 30%, investors are unlikely to complain, at least for now.

SNOW ended the quarter with $4 billion of cash and $755 million of long term investments versus $2.3 billion of convertible notes, representing a strong balance sheet. While the company is not yet profitable on a GAAP basis, the positive FCF generation leaves me little reason to be concerned about financial solvency.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see up 27% YoY product revenue growth in the first quarter and full year. I expect both those targets to prove conservative.

On the conference call, management was optimistic with regards to their positioning in the age of agentic AI due to the large volume of data generated by agents. Management noted that they have increasing conviction in Snowflake Intelligence, as it is now “at the forefront of how you use agentic AI to massively accelerate how a data platform is being used.”

Is SNOW Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

At recent prices, SNOW traded at around 7x forward sales. Consensus estimates call for around 20% top-line growth for the years to come.

That valuation represents a large - very large - premium to many large software peers like Salesforce (CRM) which trade in the 2x to 4x sales range. One can make an argument that the magnitude of the premium is not justified. Honestly, I don’t have a clear rebuttal to that claim other than to suggest that it is beyond the scope of a report focused on SNOW. When compared against peers benefitting from AI such as Cloudflare (NET), which trades at around 20x sales, SNOW looks more like a bargain.

I suspect many investors are looking in the software sector for potential bargains but are not looking to become heroes or contrarians versus AI. SNOW appears to present a compelling pick under such pretenses, as this is a company which looks poised to see its usage-based pricing model thrive as AI agents need more data and need to do more with that data - both of which stand to benefit SNOW. For now, I could buy the argument that customers are not eager to vibe-code their own data lake solutions or move to newer competitors, as data management is the kind of thing where razor-precision matters more than saving a few bucks. SNOW is still not yet profitable on a GAAP basis and it hasn’t really made much progress in the past year.

I do note that it did generate a 23% incremental GAAP operating margin in the most recent quarter. I expect the company to hit 30% net margins over the long term. The lack of profitability at this scale may make such a target look outlandish, but I am of the view that the company’s position as sort of “data middle man” can be thought of similarly to other toll road businesses such as credit cards or e-commerce take rates. As SNOW sees its business continue growing due to the explosion of data, I would expect it to be able to post robust profit margins if and when management decides to allow operating leverage to take hold. I would also expect the company to be a beneficiary of any potential operational efficiencies driven by AI given the large fixed-cost-structure.

That places the stock at around 23x long term earnings. Against the forward 26% growth rate, that represents a roughly 1x price to earnings growth ratio ("PEG ratio"). I’d prefer a lower multiple due to both the aforementioned lack of present profitability as well as the distressed levels at which many software peers trade at, but current valuations look more than reasonable for what I expect to be a top tier growth name over the coming years.

SNOW Stock Key Risks

It is possible that AI-native competitors emerge to compete on price. Perhaps SNOW begins making too much money that its industry becomes a clear target for cost savings. It is possible that AI agents do not really need so much data or are able to utilize the data without paying SNOW revenues. SNOW is not yet profitable and thus may experience heightened volatility in market downturns.

SNOW Stock Conclusion

SNOW is in the right place at the right time. As AI agents become more and more prevalent, I expect SNOW to be a direct beneficiary due to its data management solutions. The valuation looks more than reasonable in light of the aggressive forward growth rates. SNOW is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading the stock to a Buy rating.

data.title: IonQ's Edge Over Rigetti Widens, But Market Timing Delays Pay Off
data.description:
In December last year, I had recommended a pair trade, going long IonQ (IONQ) and short Rigetti Computing (RGTI). Looking back, I am more happy with the call than not because the core principle of the pair trade has worked out exactly for reasons why a pair was advised - both have collapsed by ~42%. What has not yet worked out is the alpha expected from IonQ.

My December thesis, of course, recognized difficulties in practically implementing a pair trade in two stocks that have historically been volatile, especially true for the short leg. I had hinted at several mechanisms ranging from different position sizing for the two legs and options hedges too. Depending on how positions were set up in December, the pair trade could be in significant losses too. But the core idea of not going naked long in the quantum space seems to be still directionally correct.

This thesis reviews whether the relative Buy call on IonQ still holds after Q4 earnings updates and how narratives have evolved for both the players. From that perspective, IonQ seems to be even more exposed to narratives and also moved up further in the quantum positioning stack. So, the relative call favoring IonQ has been strengthened even further in my view. I recommend no changes to positions set up in December. The relative alpha in IonQ should show up when the market conditions align and long duration growth is back in fashion.

Thematic Beta Dominant

Over the past 4 months since my thesis, the quantum computing thematic beta has been stronger. IonQ and Rigetti have almost moved in sync on the way down. A look at the historical prices and the ratio shows that this has not been the case throughout history. There has been a heavy correlation between the two throughout, but there were periods of dispersion, material enough to make individual fundamentals matter more than segment-level dynamics (like we are seeing now).

The ratio chart reveals very important takeaways for the pair trade. IonQ and Rigetti have charted their own relative course based on their own fundamentals/narratives more heavily at the start of the AI and futuristic tech themes-based rally towards the end of 2024. This was the start of a non-stop and sharp rally in quantum stocks across the board. The rally continued in 2025, but the dispersion has been less severe than in the previous regimes. Rigetti has settled far higher in terms of share prices through this period of increasing investor interest in quantum computing. However, despite lower dispersion, we still saw enough to make pair trades work until late 2025.

That was when the long-duration tech theses started getting challenged across several themes. Hyperscalers were questioned on capex and future AI economics. AI infrastructure long duration assumptions were challenged because markets became wary of demand being pulled forward and an imminent trough, especially as the capex cycle slows. SaaS has collapsed because of uncertainties around future pricing power in an evolving world powered by AI capabilities. Quantum computing is much further out in terms of economics than any of these themes but has suffered after riding strong narratives in 2024-25 in a market that was hungry for growth. When the growth markets relatively dried up (late 2025), the IonQ-Rigetti ratio became more stable - dispersion and relative positioning mattered less as markets removed valuation excesses.

Narratives, Fundamentals - IonQ and Rigetti

IonQ has materially changed its positioning narrative as per commentary in the Q4 earnings call. IonQ is no longer describing a single product - it is now talking about a full-stack quantum platform across computing, networking, sensing, and security. It sees itself as a merchant supplier to the quantum ecosystem and is also talking about large national and defense use cases (SHIELD, sovereign deployments). That is a step change from my December thesis that saw IonQ as a better fidelity and path to commercialization play.

In addition to the larger positioning as a foundational layer for the quantum ecosystem, IonQ continued to reinforce its performance leadership. Compared to leading superconducting systems, IonQ is 1000x faster in terms of reaching solutions and 10000x faster in signal processing and optimization now. Commercially too, IonQ is already talking about demand exceeding supply amidst rapid global deployments. Whether the technology and commercialization is fully proven or not is a secondary point of debate here. We are talking about a company that is further in the growth stage and more importantly, expanding its narrative surface area meaningfully every quarter.

Numbers also back IonQ's progress. The revenue reported for the full year 2025 was ~$130m, with ~$62m in Q4 alone. The exit run rate, along with remaining performance obligations of ~$370m, makes the guidance of ~$235m for 2026 more than achievable. IonQ should start getting judged on the basis of financials sooner rather than later, given predictable progress and traction.

Rigetti, on the other hand, keeps the narrative momentum down relatively. It positions itself for at least ~3 years to reach a position of real quantum advantage in what it calls a research-driven market today. The performance claims are well qualified and realistic, in my view. The company talks about controlled settings when talking about the 99.9% 2-qubit fidelity achieved. Rigetti has also transparently highlighted coupling issues at scale, delayed deployments, and redesigning systems before release. Real utility is unlikely before 2027, as by management's own estimates, it will take that much time to 1000+ qubits at ~99.8% fidelity - something that is still in the vicinity of real system requirements.

The narrative alpha in Rigetti is looking far lower, though progress is not concerning. The commercial traction is also a stage behind IonQ's and the gap has widened in Q4 (~$1.9m). There are some key contracts that could be recognized in the near term, notably a ~$8.4m system for India and ~$5.7m for small systems. But overall the gap has not shrunk, as IonQ is almost looking to double revenue in 2026, off a higher base.

Overall, the commercial numbers could continue to be a second order factor in early stage dynamics, but IonQ's predictable financial trajectory now gives more confidence to investors. The performance and commercial gaps have widened in favor of IonQ, but they were already visible in December. What has changed more meaningfully, in my view, is IonQ's platform and ecosystem positioning. That introduces new revenue opportunities but also means higher capital intensity and broader execution risks.

Reviewing the Pair Trade

Overall, both from a narrative and fundamentals perspective, IonQ's superiority is obvious in my view - strengthened after the Q4 call. That means IonQ will need the growth markets to return to be able to outperform Rigetti. I had initially expected several months to even a couple of years for this pair trade to truly work out and that view remains. More so as the growth markets are now subdued and IonQ needs markets with long duration appetite given its narrative heavy and capital intensive position. Rigetti too can benefit from the high growth narrative, but in early tech, I believe, more certainty and progress, is rewarded earlier. Speculative bursts in Rigetti have already happened over the past year - the IonQ-Rigetti price ratio is near historical lows. So, IonQ's further progress and commercial traction should help the stock to pull away from Rigetti in conducive market conditions.

Despite my tilt toward IonQ, where I retain a Buy (and Rigetti a Sell), I don't think it will be prudent to take naked positions in IonQ simply because a ~42% correction has happened. Switching to the stronger leg is often a good move in pair trades, but not in early stage growth bets, which have already rallied by leaps and bounds in the recent past. Despite cooling, IonQ is still 4x the share price levels seen at the start of the rally around September 2024 (that explosive growth is even more pronounced in the case of Rigetti at ~14x September 2024 levels). So, further downside is still a possibility - both stocks could still settle well above the 2024 levels even after further material corrections, still acknowledging progress made relative to 2024. In short, we are closer to a naked long position in IonQ today, but the pair trade remains the best way to express this relative pecking order.

I expect IonQ and Rigetti to remain very correlated on the way down, especially if led by a weakening growth market and repricing the quantum theme as a whole continues to be the dominant factor. The fundamental factors should start playing out in favor of IonQ once the corrections are over, and better still if growth appetite returns. Until then, I'd treat quantum as one trade and keep the pair positioning intact.

data.title: Targa Resources: This Stock Has Performed Well, But It Still Has Potential Today
data.description:
Targa Resources (TRGP) is a natural gas and natural gas liquids-focused midstream company that primarily owns and operates a network of energy infrastructure that includes natural gathering pipelines, natural gas processing facilities, natural gas liquids pipelines, and natural gas liquids fractionation facilities. The company has been one of the most rapidly growing midstream companies in the United States ever since the COVID-19 pandemic shocked the industry, which has allowed it to deliver a very impressive stock performance over the past five years. Fortunately for investors in this company, Targa Resources appears likely to continue to deliver growth going forward, which should set investors up for significant returns. The downside here is that Targa Resources only has a 1.63% current yield, which is rather low for the midstream sector. This may discourage investors whose primary interest is in generating income from their portfolios, but for investors who wish to maximize their total return, Targa Resources could be a reasonable option.

About Targa Resources

Targa Resources is a natural gas and natural gas liquids-focused midstream company that operates natural gas gathering pipelines, natural gas processing plants, long-haul natural gas liquids pipelines, and natural gas liquids fractionation facilities. Targa Resources primarily operates in the Permian Basin of western Texas and eastern New Mexico, but it also has operations in Oklahoma and in North Dakota’s Badlands region:

The North Dakota Badlands are a rugged region of western North Dakota that consists of a number of buttes, ravines, canyons, and mesas that were carved out of the terrain by wind and the waterflows of the Little Missouri River. As far as the oil and gas industry is concerned, though, this region is a resource-rich hydrocarbon region that includes the Bakken and Three Fork Shales. Targa Resources has approximately 500 miles of crude oil gathering pipelines and 205,000 barrels of crude oil storage capacity in the region. The company also has 300 miles of natural gas gathering pipelines and three natural gas processing plants in the area. The company also has an ownership interest in a fourth natural gas processing plant that is larger than the others, but its interest in this one is through a 50/50 joint venture with Hess Midstream (HESM).

As is the case with many midstream companies, though, Targa Resources’ operations in the Permian Basin are significantly larger than its operations in any other region of the United States. Targa Resources did not break down its revenues by region in its most recent earnings report, which corresponds to the fourth quarter of 2025. However, its natural gas processing plants in the Permian Basin processed approximately 6.6507 billion cubic feet of natural gas per day in the fourth quarter of 2025. That was 79.51% of the company’s nationwide total of 8.3649 billion cubic feet of natural gas per day. Furthermore, its production of natural gas liquids in the Permian Basin was an average of 937,700 barrels per day, which was 84.68% of its nationwide total of 1.1073 million barrels of natural gas liquids per day. Thus, the Permian region, which includes the company’s operations in both the Delaware and Midland Basins, is by far the most important for its financial performance.

It is therefore quite a good thing for Targa Resources that natural gas production has been growing in the Permian Basin. According to the U.S. Energy Information Administration, the Permian Basin only accounted for 23% of the total production of natural gas in the United States. While this is less than the Appalachian region produced, it is still a much larger percentage than the Permian Basin was producing ten years ago:

We can clearly see that the section of the chart that shows the natural gas production of the Permian Basin has been widening significantly over the past ten years. That particular basin has also been growing more rapidly than other regions of the United States. We see this in 2024, for example:

Basin

Clearly, we can see that the production of natural gas in the Permian Basin grew significantly more than the production of natural gas in either of the other two regions. This is not particularly surprising, which is partly due to the Permian Basin having the best economics of the three. In Appalachia, for example, the quantity and capacity of midstream pipelines are rather limited, and as such, it is very difficult for producers in that region to get natural gas to the market. While the Mountain Valley Pipeline, which began operations in the middle of 2024, increased the takeaway capacity in the area by quite a bit, the current production of the region is still coming very close to filling all of the pipelines that exist in the area. As pipelines are the only way to transport natural gas over land that makes any economic sense at all, there is no reason for upstream companies that operate in the area to increase their production when they would not be able to get the natural gas to a place where it could be sold. The Haynesville Shale is more expensive for producers to operate in than either Appalachia or the Permian Basin. This is due to the fact that the Haynesville formation is 10,500 to 13,500 feet deep, which means that companies need to drill deeper wells to access the natural gas in that area than is the case in Appalachia (which requires wells of 4,000 to 8,500 feet to access). Generally speaking, the deeper the well, the more expensive it is to obtain the resources, which means that companies operating in the Haynesville Shale generally have to accept lower profit margins. This reduces the incentive to produce resources in that area (although it is still profitable to do so with Henry Hub natural gas prices at today’s levels). Admittedly, there are parts of the Permian Basin that are even deeper than the Haynesville Shale. The Woodford and Barnett shales, for example, are up to 20,000 feet deep. However, the Permian Basin has the advantage of crude oil also being present, which allows companies operating in that region to drill for both resources. At today’s oil prices, the crude oil in the Permian Basin is profitable to extract (at least in certain parts of the Permian), so companies in that area mostly target the oil and receive the natural gas as a byproduct. While there are other regions (such as the Williston Basin in North Dakota’s Badlands) that also have crude oil, these other plays require oil prices to consistently be above $80 per barrel to attract much production activity. Thus, the Permian Basin is the only region that is seeing much production growth due to it being the only region that truly makes sense to operate in.

Targa Resources currently has one of the largest natural gas gathering pipeline and natural gas processing networks in the Permian Basin. Natural gas gathering pipelines are vital for the production of natural gas, as these are the pipelines that grab the natural gas as it comes out of the well and transport it to either a larger natural gas long-haul pipeline or to a natural gas processing plant. Natural gas is, as the name implies, a gas, and, as such, it expands to fill any container that it is placed in. This makes it very difficult to economically transport via any means other than a pipeline that is equipped with pumping stations to keep the natural gas flowing through the pipelines. This is a big reason why natural gas was flared in the Permian Basin ten years ago (and earlier), as there were not sufficient natural gas gathering pipelines in the area to capture the gas and actually move it through all of the steps necessary to make it marketable. The production of natural gas in the Permian Basin has grown at a 12% compound annual growth rate over the past six years, which has naturally resulted in growing volumes of natural gas needing to be collected by companies such as Targa Resources that own and operate natural gas gathering pipelines. Furthermore, natural gas needs to be processed before it can actually be used due to the fact that natural gas has a lot of impurities in it when it comes out of the ground. As the production of natural gas increased, the amount of natural gas that needs to be processed has also increased. Targa Resources has been able to take advantage of this, as Targa Resources has grown the amount of natural gas moving through its gathering and processing infrastructure in the Permian Basin at a 16% compound annual growth rate over the past six years:

As we can see, Targa Resources has managed to grow the volume of resources moving through the company’s gathering and processing systems at a faster pace than the actual output of natural gas in the Permian Basin. This is mostly just because the company’s facilities (gathering pipelines and processing plants) are located in close proximity to the specific places in which the production growth is occurring.

The reason why this is a good thing for Targa Resources is due to the business model that the company employs. As is the case with nearly all midstream companies, Targa Resources charges a fee for each unit of hydrocarbon products (crude oil, natural gas, or natural gas liquids) that moves through its infrastructure network. In fact, roughly 90% of the company’s projected operating margins for 2026 come from these fees:

This is very different from the way that an upstream oil or gas company makes its money. Unlike those firms, Targa Resources has very little exposure to energy prices. The fees that it charges to its customers do not change based on the price of resources. The company does have a small amount of exposure to movements in commodity prices, but it has hedges in place to prevent its cash flows from fluctuating too much from movements in commodity prices. For the most part, though, we can clearly see that growth in the amount of resources that move through Targa Resources’ infrastructure results in revenue and cash flow growth. For this reason, then, Targa Resources should benefit from resource production increases in the regions in which it operates. As we just saw, that has very much been the case over the past several years, and this has been a driver of growth for the company.

The fact that Targa Resources’ financial performance is more closely linked to volumes than to resource prices, the company tends to enjoy relative stability with respect to its financial performance. This is even more true when we consider that Targa Resources primarily conducts its business operations under long-term contracts that typically include provisions such as minimum volume commitments that ensure that the firm’s customers have to pay for a specific volume of resources even if they do not transport this quantity. This protects Targa Resources’ cash flows if an energy price collapse prompts upstream producers to reduce their output. This results in the company having relatively stable cash flows over time, which we can see by looking at Targa Resources’ operating cash flows over the past nine years:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

We can see that, for every one of the past nine years, Targa Resources’ operating cash flows came in higher than was the case in the previous year. This was even true for 2020, when the COVID-19 pandemic caused a massive drop in crude oil prices and a corresponding drop in crude oil production:

This chart shows the average crude oil production for the United States during every month over the past ten years. We can see a steep decline in April 2020, and then an even steeper decline in May 2020, which corresponds to the worst months of the pandemic-driven oil price decline (allowing for a slight lag). Furthermore, crude oil production in the United States did not reach its pre-pandemic peak of approximately 13 million barrels per day until August 2023. Thus, production was actually lower than its peak for more than three years after crude oil prices crashed. Despite this, Targa Resources’ operating cash flows never declined and continued to increase despite the lower production. Many risk-averse income investors will likely find this to be an attractive characteristic for the company since it should reduce the overall risks faced by investors.

With that said, Targa Resources’s infrastructure, particularly in the Permian Basin, is more focused on the transportation and processing of natural gas instead of crude oil. This is important because natural gas production in the Permian held up much better than crude oil production did when prices collapsed in 2020. In fact, the annual average natural gas production in the Permian Basin increased in every year over the 2013 to 2023 period:

While we do see that natural gas production did decline in early-mid 2020 when upstream companies shut in their production and reduced output, the 2020 average was still higher than the 2019 average. We saw the same thing occur in 2021, which saw the average production come in higher than the 2020 figure. Targa Resources makes its money based on volumes, so this was still conducive to growth through the pandemic period. In this case, then, the fact that Targa Resources focuses primarily on natural gas and natural gas liquids was an advantage for it.

Targa Resources’ transported volumes also did not decline very much during the pandemic era despite the steep decline in U.S. rig counts, drilling activity, and crude oil production. In fact, as we can see here, the company’s transported volumes in the Permian Basin have pretty consistently trended upwards for fifteen years:

While there were a few small declines in Targa’s volumes over the years, they all proved temporary and very short-lived. Following each decline, Targa Resources very quickly managed to recover the lost volumes and resume growth. This is consistent with the growth in the company’s cash flow, which also has been consistently increasing from year to year.

Over the 2011 to 2026 period, Targa Resources managed to grow the amount of natural gas that it handles at a 20.67% compound annual growth rate. As we have already seen, this has been quite good for the company’s operating cash flow over time. It has also been very good for the company’s stock price. Over the past ten years, Targa Resources has seen its stock price increase by 646.36%, substantially outperforming the S&P 500 Index (SP500):

As was mentioned in the introduction, Targa Resources has a 1.63% yield at the current price. This is rather low compared to many other midstream companies:

Company Name

Current Yield

Targa Resources

3.94%

ONEOK (OKE)

Targa Resources has the lowest yield of any of the major midstream companies shown here, and indeed, it has one of the lowest yields of any company in the midstream sector in general. However, its yield is still higher than that of the S&P 500 Index. After all, the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) only yields 1.09% at the current level. This means that Targa Resources’ historical performance advantage over the S&P 500 Index should be even more pronounced if we assume that investors reinvested the distributions and dividends over the period. This is indeed the case:

Over the past ten years, Targa Resources has delivered a 1,075.57% total return, easily trouncing the S&P 500 Total Return Index (SP500TR). This is one of the few midstream companies to have achieved that level of performance. After all, as many regular readers know, the energy sector as a whole has not been an especially good creator of wealth over the past decade.

Forward Growth Prospects

As we just saw, Targa Resources has been a very good performer over the past ten years. After all, it has delivered both cash flow growth and strong stock performance. However, anyone who is purchasing the stock today will not benefit from the stock’s performance in the past. The important thing for anyone buying today is how well the company will perform going forward. Let us take a look at this.

Fortunately, Targa Resources does have some projects in the works that should prove beneficial for the company’s profitability and cash flow going forward. Alongside the company’s fourth-quarter 2025 earnings report, Targa Resources announced that it is beginning construction on a new natural gas processing plant in the Permian Basin. This plant has been named Yeti II, and it has a design capacity of 275 million cubic feet of natural gas per day. This new plant is in addition to the other three that Targa Resources currently has under construction in the Permian Basin:

Plant Name

Estimated Date of Operation

Falcon II

Q1 2026

Copperhead

Q1 2027

Yeti

Q3 2027

Yeti II

Q4 2027

Immediately, we notice that Falcon II was supposed to start operating sometime in the first three months of 2026. In other words, if the company managed to complete it on schedule, then it should already be in operation. However, no announcement has been made as of yet as to whether or not that plant has already begun operations. In the fourth quarter 2025 earnings report, Targa Resources stated:

We expect to bring online our Falcon II plant in the Permian Delaware ahead of schedule, now in the first quarter of 2026.

This statement was made on February 19, 2026, and there are no updates listed on the company’s own news page regarding this project as of April 10, 2026. As such, we do not know if this plant is already operational or not. If Targa Resources did manage to get it working, then it should provide a slight boost to Targa Resources’ first quarter 2026 earnings results, although its contribution to the second quarter’s cash flows will be greater due to spending more time operational in the second quarter than during the first. However, until Targa Resources provides an update, we have no way of knowing for sure if this is how that particular scenario will play out.

The remainder of the three natural gas processing plants that the company has under construction will provide it with some growth over the course of 2027 and 2028. As we can see above, the plants are scheduled to begin operations in the first, third, and fourth quarters of that year. In many cases, it is the quarter following the one in which the project is actually completed that sees the greatest performance impact due to the project having more time to operate during that quarter. Thus, we will see positive impacts from these projects through the first quarter of 2028, assuming that Targa Resources manages to get them operational on schedule. Historically, this company has been pretty good about getting projects completed either on schedule or ahead of schedule, so we can probably assume that this will be the case this time as well.

One of the nicest things about all three of these projects is that Targa Resources has already secured commitments from customers that ensure that this capacity will be used. Thus, we can be confident that the firm is not spending a great deal of money to construct new infrastructure that is not needed and that will not be profitable.

Targa Resources also appears to be preparing to construct an additional two natural gas processing plants in addition to the ones mentioned above. However, the company has not named them, nor has it provided any specific production timetables or other information about them. We know this because it has ordered some of the equipment that would be needed to outfit two natural gas processing plants, and the company does not actually need this equipment for any of the plants that it has already announced. Thus, we should probably expect to see two more Permian natural gas plants announced at some point in the future, which will contribute to the company’s growth post-2027.

Finally, Targa Resources has a fairly large project that should prove to be a significant source of growth beginning around the end of 2027. This is the Speedway NGL Pipeline, which is a 500-mile-long natural gas liquids pipeline that runs from the Permian Basin to Mont Belvieu, Texas, where Targa Resources hosts the bulk of its natural gas liquids fractionation capacity:

The Speedway NGL Pipeline is the dotted line in this map.

This pipeline will have an initial capacity of about 500,000 barrels of natural gas liquids per day. As of right now, Targa Resources has a total natural gas liquids transportation capacity of roughly 1,000,000 barrels per day (it was exactly 1.0487 million in Q4 2025), so the Speedway Pipeline will increase Targa Resources’ transportation capacity by 50% once it begins operations. However, the potential growth from this pipeline could be even greater than that. This is because the Speedway Pipeline is an expandable pipeline, which means that its capacity can be increased as needed to accommodate growing customer needs. In the case of the Speedway NGL Pipeline, the capacity can be increased to as high as one million barrels of natural gas liquids per day:

If the energy industry’s upstream production grows enough that this full capacity is needed, it would double the volume of natural gas liquids flowing through Targa Resources’ infrastructure. Targa Resources’ Logistics and Transportation Segment, which includes all of the company’s natural gas liquids transportation and fractionation activities, reported an operating margin of $799.0 million for the fourth quarter of 2025. With Speedway coming online in the third quarter of 2027, along with the fractionation trains that Targa Resources is adding to its Mont Belvieu processing facilities, it is not difficult to see a scenario in which the company’s Logistics and Transportation Segment has an operating margin of around $1.4 billion heading into 2028.

For the fourth quarter of 2025, Targa Resources had an operating margin of $1.4108 billion between its two business segments. The Speedway NGL Pipeline and new fractionation units alone should get this up to over $2 billion by 2028, and that is not even including the new natural gas processing plants. With those plants, it is not difficult to see a scenario in which the company grows at a roughly 20%-25% compound annual growth rate over the next two years. However, the vast majority of this growth will come in 2027. The company’s management is only expecting adjusted EBITDA to grow at a 10.55% rate in 2026, and given the start-up schedule for Targa Resources’ current projects, I am inclined to agree. It is 2027 that will be the big growth year for this company, not 2026.

Recent Performance

The last time that we discussed Targa Resources here on Seeking Alpha was on September 14, 2025. Subscribers to Energy Profits in Dividends have gotten more frequent updates on this company. As such, about seven months have passed since we last looked at this company. The company has delivered a very impressive performance since that date, as the stock price is up a very impressive 49.85%, compared to only 3.17% for the S&P 500 Index:

In terms of total return, the company’s performance has been even more impressive, as shareholders of Targa Resources have benefited from a total return of 51.60%, which was far better than what the S&P 500 Index delivered over the same period:

Admittedly, some of this may be due to the surge in oil prices that has occurred since the end of February. Targa Resources is an S&P 500 Index constituent, and as such, it is included in the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) as well as other exchange-traded funds that are designed to passively track the stock prices of companies in the energy sector. It is somewhat common for momentum traders or swing traders to purchase energy equity ETFs whenever oil prices are moving upwards on the expectation that oil and gas companies will see higher profits (which is, admittedly, usually the case). As Targa Resources is included in these funds, its common stock also experiences buying pressure when such events occur even though Targa Resources itself does not directly benefit from rising energy prices. Thus, the surge in crude oil prices that started with the outbreak of the Iran War was one driver of this strong stock performance. However, as we can see, Targa Resources was still delivering strong gains even before this occurred. Thus, while the company’s stock was a beneficiary of rising prices, that was not the only reason why the prices rose. As we have already seen, Targa Resources has also been delivering earnings and cash flow growth, so that also contributed to the stock’s strong performance over the period.

Valuation

As of the time of writing, Targa Resources has a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 24.45. This is actually not particularly out of line with many peer midstream companies, despite the very strong stock market performance that Targa Resources has delivered over the past few years. We can see that in this comparison chart:

Company Name

Forward P/E Ratio

Targa Resources

24.45

The Williams Companies

31.78

Kinder Morgan

23.98

Enbridge

25.36

Pembina Pipeline

22.19

TC Energy

23.78

ONEOK

15.33

The Williams Companies is by far the most expensive company in this group of seven midstream companies. However, all six of the remaining companies are trading in the 22-25.50 range, so none of them appears to be particularly expensive relative to each other.

In addition, the S&P 500 Index has a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 23.60 right now:

Targa Resources is slightly above this, but not by enough to be worrying. As such, the company does not really appear to be overpriced right now.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant System, however, does seem to suggest that Targa Resources is a bit expensive right now. This system shows that the company is priced well above the energy sector median on a variety of metrics and has assigned the company a valuation grade of “F”:

Here is how that valuation grade compares to the midstream peers:

Company Name

This system does certainly suggest that Targa Resources is somewhat overpriced compared to its peers. However, that certainly does not mean that investors should start dumping their shares. Indeed, given the growth prospects here, it is very likely that Targa Resources will continue to perform reasonably well for at least the next few years. The main drawback here is that an investor buying Targa Resources today will pay a bit more for the company’s growth than they would with another midstream company (although, granted, most of the peer midstream companies have lower growth potential.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Targa Resources still looks like either a weak “Buy” or a very strong “Hold” right now. It is not a screaming buy at the current valuation, but it does not appear to be especially expensive. The company is also likely to deliver respectable growth over the next few years, and that certainly justifies holding on to the stock if you already have it. Indeed, it is not a horrible idea to purchase more shares today, but investors should know that they are paying a bit more for this company than for some of the other midstream companies in the market.
